speaker: Robert Hieronimus
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: That's when I think most people forget, while you're donating your time to pay for these things and to set up the different, whether it's going to be an auction, whether it's going to be a show or something like that, it's an immense amount of money to pay other people to do your work and that's never counted. I mean, that's the most expensive part. It comes into, I hate to get them because whenever I've discussed this with the general family, most of the people within the family thought we were crazy, they thought we were fools. They would say, who's paying you to do this? I mean, you know, when I work at something and I get paid $150-$200 an hour and I'm giving not like 10 hours, I'm giving two months straight of using every chip, you know what chit, it's me, you know, using every angle and every, everyone that ever owed me anything. I would go to them and say, look, I did this for you, I did that for you and that and that. I don't want anything for myself, but what about can you do this? So you use up all those favors, which means that three years later, when you need them again, you can't call them on it, nothing is free in politics and especially in the corporate world, things that you do for other people are never forgotten, costs are not forgotten. You know, I wish I would have known this when I was growing up. I thought people did things out of it because they loved doing it with all the goodness of their heart or that it was the right thing to do. Seth, that's not how it was. How it was is that, okay, Bob, I'll do that, I'll get that on there, but how about in return I have this big house and I got lots of wall space for paintings. Now I can't afford your work, but you know, then they'd let it drop. You pick up the ball. Okay, what do you want? What do you want? They won't spell it out. You come over to my house and my studio and I'll show you around with the full intention of knowing that when they get to a certain part of your studio, they're going to look at your paintings, but it's never spoken. Oh, I like that. You like that a lot? Oh, I like that very much. Oh, okay. Well, I'll tell you what, why don't you take it with you? Okay, I'll take this with me. Okay, you can be sure that, oh yes, of course we'll do that on the phone. We'll make the phone calls, we'll do that. You know, unfortunately, most people who did not go into these rat infested, roach infested, no food, cold, squalor of places, and this wasn't Leon's place, these were other places that I had gone into. How in a world can you not want to help these people? You know, we humans are like that. We all feel that when you're in a situation like that, it's up to you to do something about it, whether it's sweeping their floor or setting their chairs up straight or pouring them a cup of coffee, you feel like you got to do something. And unfortunately, that's dying in this country and, you know, because everyone's thinking that when you get back, because we're going to run out of time if we don't do it, when we get back to this alleged pension, of course the people who really talk about this are the vice president and the president of NLBPA, they're the only really official speakers on that. And that is Wilmer Fields and Stanley Glenn. But what we had thought was going to happen was this. We had fought since, because these guys have been hammering at baseball for a long time, you know, the organization baseball. And we had thought that they would eventually cave in and say, okay, enough's enough. You've proven that, you know, you had guys that hit 80 home runs a year, you had guys that stole, could steal our base runners blind and strike out whole sides of our teams, you know, and all that kind of thing. So, all right. So we expected them to get a kind of like a pension, which would be set up of several thousand to hopefully ten thousand, fifteen thousand, something like that a year, even if it was five thousand. And that they were supposed to be all the living Negro League players. Now, you're going to run into this sooner or later if you haven't already. What is a Negro League player? A Negro League player is not a black ball player. A Negro League player according to the NLBPA is a player that played in the Negro Leads from 1920 to no later than 1950. Now, many of the people that are alive today that are still, that were players and didn't they played on some Negro League teams, but that doesn't mean they were Negro League players. They weren't major league players. There's a big hassle that's been going on forever and it would never stop. It would never stop because all of a sudden some, some body who played on a black ball team for a year or two, is elevated to the position of Leon Day. Well, that would be the same thing as if you look at back in here as, oh, I don't know, Sam Maus used to play a pitch for the Cleveland Indians in the fifties. You match him up to a Hoyt Wilhelm or something. I mean, you know, there's a lot of difference between greatness and good enough to make him age or needs. And that's the same with the Negro League players. They felt very strongly that the people that should receive these pensions, if you could call them that, would have to be Negro League players. Well, when the Negro League, excuse me, black ball players got wind of money coming. Well, it all helped loose because it drew the worst type of feelings out of individuals that felt like they had been taking advantage of all their life. And I got to see a dark side to some of these black ball players, which group was greed. Not that they had much money. Not that they had anything. But if that guy, if Roma fields or, say, the on day or Stanley Glen was getting so much money for playing 20 years or 15 years in the Negro League players teams, then all these other players felt like they deserved the same thing. That doesn't work that way. It never has worked that way. But that is what has been a major problem now. And so that the problem is this, you have a minimum of 100, excuse me, a maximum of 170 Negro League players that played in the major leagues alive. Chances are it's probably closer to 150. They die six to ten years. So you can imagine, and that's escalating. So it's not going to be like six to ten years. It's going to be like, and within ten years, it's all over. They aren't going to be any of these players, whether they were major league, minor league, mixed no difference. So that difference in trying to get funds for those 170, we only succeeded in getting funds for 70. And the rest of the 100 who were bonafide were dropped. Now there were a lot of black ball players that played for ten years, but they played 1950 to 1965, somewhere around and there. And the competition was, it was pedally. That's what the major league players were truly unhappy about. The teams that they played against from the 20s to 1947 were the toughest ever. They were just playing tough. And you know, when they look at from 1950, all of them were just, they would say, not even minor league. They were called semi-pro. How would you say that your involvement with the Negro league players has affected the Baltimore community? Oh, the Baltimore community. I don't know how it's affected the Baltimore community. I know how it's affected the national community. See we've never had much help here in Baltimore. It's really strange. Most of the like some of the articles you'll see here written in the Dallas Times and then they were mostly national because I was talking to well educated sports casters, guys that had, believe it or not, there were not any broadcasters, but they had their bachelor's and masters in the area of broadcasting services. They were intelligent. So when they got a book, they read it. So we would send them a book on the history of the Negro league. And I was so naive to think, oh, I want to send this $35 book to this person. A week later, I get a call back from a guy in Dallas and he's like, damn, it's the greatest gift I ever got. I just finished it. I'm talking about my sons and my dad and dad and that. Well, you can imagine how it feels to finally attract those types. Mel Allen was his name. That's who it was. You know, I heard him Mel Allen. When I mentioned, he was from New York, Yankees. He was one of the guys that used to think Negro leaders didn't do too much then after he was educated. We realized that they were just tops, you know, at least equal to. Anyway, the Baltimore community, what we have done is we have literally been able to bring a certain part of the Blacks history, which they are very proud of into the mainstream. And so that we have certainly affected the Afro-American papers. Baltimore son papers, the help that we've received there was not from the male sports people. It was the female in the son papers that were writing in the sports section. They were the people that stood up for us. And the reason why they stood up for was they said, this is a human situation. They hit it right on the head. How can you have a player go out on the field and play for a million dollars a year who's a Black player and that's not very much these days, is it? How can you have that guy go out there and do that, built on the backs of all these other Black players that made it possible for him to do so? To me, you've got to be pretty much of a slob. You've got to be pretty much of a pig to do that to other humans. But unfortunately, people like Reggie Jackson and others are like that. You know, it was very unfortunate from that standpoint. So as far as Baltimore goes, I think we educated the general community. We certainly have not endeared herself to the sports people. Are there any anecdotes that it's to go out in your mind from any of the players that you can readily remember collecting? Oh, yeah. Most of the things that touched me deeply is when we took these guys to the White House, and then we took them to the White House twice. And it was all the way to the White House. I mean, you have to understand these guys have never been recognized for anything. And to go to the White House was the biggest dream they've ever had. I mean, none of them ever fought. None of them ever fought, but this would ever happen. And on the way there, they would say, imagine this. I'm going to go on to the White House. I'm going to tell my wife all about it. I'm like, my grandchildren, all of his grandchildren, they're going to have a big party when they come back home so they can share what the White House was like. Grandpa's going to the White House. Well, then it's then that he started to reminisce. And they'd say, a whole discussion's of, do you remember that game? Or when we were on the way to someplace in the South, and the bus turned over? And a lot of people were hurt, but no one was really savagely hurt. And we put a phone call in and saying, hey, we're in real trouble here. We're hurt. The bus turned in the first thing, the owner of the team says to them was, hey, you've got a half hour to get to the game. Get to the game. That's extraordinary. Of course, there were major league players that played with broken thumbs. And these guys, praying with broken ribs all the time, they couldn't, they had that kind of pressure. Or when they were hungry, this is the stuff that really got me because Seth, I come from a very poor background. Food has always been important to me. I ate well when I was younger, but when I left home on my own, about when I was about 19. And I went through a period of time in which I had won me a little day for years. I mean, that's the way it was. So they would tell stories about the places they would go to eat, which I think was really an incredible experience. They would go to, of course, they could never go into front door. They go around the back door, but they still had to go through the situation in which the people behind the counter, even though they were feeding them, they would give them the worst of what they had, stuff that was already bad. But one story they told, which I just loved, went kind of like this. And they went to this restaurant, restaurant, diner. Can you call who these people are telling you? Oh, yes, Monty Urban, Monty Urban, Buck Leonard, threw in a couple of stories there, but especially Monty Urban, Jean Benson, Maillam Duckett, these individuals were all involved in this particular one, Wilmer Fields. So they sent one of the guys in who had, they always sent the guy in that had the lightest complexion. He would get the less hassle. He would take the order and carry it out. So they ordered something like, I don't know, 20 hand-borders or something like that, 20 sandwiches. And so when he was at the counter and the white man looked at him and he said, okay, well, here's these sandwiches, but you can't eat them here. You have to take these out and go eat them elsewhere. And I can't recall the exact words, but what they did was this. He said, okay, you enjoy these 20 hand-borders, we're leaving. And they left. That was the funniest, the most fun they had in playing any, but usually it was just the reverse. It was usually like kind of like they would pull into a place to get a drink of water. This was Monty Urban's story again. And he would look out, he would look out back and there was a well, the water and the person, of course, wouldn't give him anything to drink, wouldn't sell them sodas and everything. And he said, well, can we have a drink from the well back there? I mean, she said, okay, I guess so. So they went back there, they used this little board of the ladle and they drank from the ladle and they were kind of hurried by this lady. They got in their cars and they looked out the window and as they were relieving, they saw her in the back smashing the board. Basically saying, I had to give these guys water, but I don't want to touch anything, these guys. Most of the traveling they did on the bus, they ate terribly. They had these, many times they had these little box lunches, if you would recall that, which each one had, it was pretty much the same thing. They would go to a place they'd buy a loaf of bread and a poundable only and some onions and few other things and maybe some drinks. And they would store these under their seats. Well, in 95 degree weather, they were eating food which was green. They were haver, but they had to. It's traveling from place to place without food was terrible, but staying in the places that they had to stay in, none of us would ever stay in places like that. Can you describe anything? They actually describe some of the places that there were so many bed bugs and other vermin within the bed that they had to leave the lights on. There was a law that they had, a rule that they had. Leave the lights on because the roaches in the bed bugs don't come out until you turn the light off because when you turn the light off, and this is what I learned when I was very poor and I used to have to sleep on the floor, if you leave the light on, the rats and the other bugs. I mean, it's not fun laying in bed and feeling roaches is crawling on you. I've done it. I know what it's like. It is a miserable feeling, you know, and they had to do it all frequently because they were sleeping in urine-infested beds in places that certainly would be condemned. And the thing that I find most irritating is that as much as I like Buck O'Neill, who's done a lot of good for the Negro League players, a lot, he has also hurt them in a sense that he has come out and said many times that they stayed in the best hotels. They ate the best food and dad and dad and dad and dad. Well, not 99 percent of them. Maybe the Kansas City monarchs did because they had things specially worked out. You know, there are people Wilkinson, who was one of the owners of that time, was very, very careful about his team. So they would put them up at the best black hotels, eating the best black restaurants. That's what Buck O'Neill neglected to say. These were not top-notch anything. They were, they may have been considered to be passable by whites, but for most blacks, it was as good as they've was ever going to get. So it was staying in hotels and, you know, the transportation thing, I think, has been talked about often. But how in a world can you stay in a bus for 16 hours straight? Get out, get to the game one time and then go immediately into playing and beating the team, then get into bus again traveling two hours to another place to go. And then getting into the bus again for a third game. They were playing three and four games a day. Why? Not because they loved it. They had to make money. And if they lost, they're not cut into their savings. So they had to keep this incessant moving. It reminds me of a lot of my friends in the rock and roll world who, when they go out on these tours, it is miserable. It's not any kind of life that anyone, anyone would choose because there's no time to do anything serious. There's no time to enjoy yourself. You just work and work and work. It was the same way with these individuals who had to, if they were strong enough, what they were going to have to do is play another game if they could. So it wasn't so much just the places where they stayed, but you can imagine hell. And the guys, you can tell me about the buses. The reason why I mentioned this thing about bus and the White House is because there was the greatest bus these guys have ever been on. WJZ gave us their VIP bus, which has lounges and places to lay down and places to eat. And then go into the White House and things like that. They would all say, can you imagine? Because some of the teams didn't have, you're going to go swimming? Some of the teams couldn't afford buses so they would use big cars. They'd have to fit 15 people into two where they can set up to be Packards or older cars to go from place to place. And these were the cars that would turn over a lot. Because they'd be riding down in the South, I understand, when they didn't have cement asphalt type roads, they would have dirt roads. And then these dirt roads, what happens is if you ride down the same ruts all the time, you have a serious problem. You have ruts along the side and this mound that builds up in the center. So if you were riding down this road and your wheels hit the wrong angle on these little hills that were in the middle, you would turn your car over. And this happened not a lot. But dozens of times, the most people would say that's a lot. But I mean, since they were traveling infinitely all the time, the southern roads were really the worst, as well as the way they were treated by the people in the towns. With the exception of just a few people, like Satjel Page, he was the only one that could get away with what he was, because he was like, whenever he came to town and pitched for a team, and I know you already know this, the team was going to make money. The whites were going to make money. The people, the concession stands were going to make money because more people would come. He was a major drawing card all the time. And of course, he would pitch some games, he would pitch one inning, some games he would pitch three innings. At the end of his career, that's what he would do. So the people would say, Satjel Page is pitching, we're going to the game. Well, he would pitch one inning and that would bring out the other individuals who may have even been better than Page, but couldn't draw. Because if you couldn't draw on those days, we didn't have any big ads, you know, you didn't have any TV. It was the people on the stands that Page are free. So you got to had to draw. If you didn't draw, it was over. I think one of the things I have learned from them, though, Seth, is we'll never see other human beings like these players ever again. Because of the way the media has played up all sports stuff. These guys were heroes in the sense that they, not only were the better than everybody else that they were playing against or at least equal to, but they knew how to take crap. They knew how to be humiliated and not show it. It's built up something within their character that we'll never see again. People aren't like that. Blacks, whites, reds, makes no difference. Not that we really should be that way, but they had this patience which no longer exists. And part of some people say, yeah, they had patience because if they did the wrong thing, they'd get their head blown off. Well, yeah, that's true. But they also had very spiritual lives. And when you go talk with Buck Leonard and Buck O'Neill and others, when they travel in these buses, Wilmer Fields and Stanley Glen and Benson and Maelan and all these other guys, they were all, they all had a spiritual, most of the ones that I had, and the affiliated and worked with, had this deep spiritual thing about Christ. No two ways about it, but they felt very close to Christ and they would sing all the time. They'd sing gospels all the time. Well, we were going to the White House. Just to remember, they were flashing back thinking, God, the bus that we had then, we had three good tires and one that we wrapped with rags, the whole together or something like that. And here we are going to the White House with a brand new VIP bus. It was just like, I think it added years on to their life because they almost all have the same health kind of problems, prostate cancer and certainly bad hearts because of their diet. You know, everything was deep fried. They were told that was good for you except for a central page. He said, you know, you never should eat this kind of stuff because it'll kill you. Kill your gun. I don't know how he learned that or who told him it. He was right. He was one of the few ball players that took care of himself physically in the kind of food he ate. What didn't mean that he was not trying to build this kind of a saint because he wasn't. He had a mean streak like everybody else and just like our president was probably a big womanizer as, you know, as a gentleman is and probably always will be. So where we go from here? I would ask you what impact has this had on your life with the new game players? Well, it's kept me in touch with what it is like never to be accepted. You see, I'm white. Being white, I can go places that other humans can't. I know that now. I've forgotten what I was like. I remembered back in the late 60s, if you ever saw pictures of me, I had a beard that was down to here. My hair was screaming all. I was considered to be being an artist at that time since you were very poor, unacceptable as a human. I know what it's like to go into places where you're beat up or things are thrown at you. You try, you know, my car was bashed up a number of times by Rednecks and whites who would see your painted car or painted auto and they think, I want to destroy this bastard. I don't care. You know, and being a small person, you're an easy hit. But I'm still white. So from that standpoint, I could get my hair cut. I could get a shave. I could drive, use ordinary transportation and I could melt right back into society. These guys never could. And it's one of the things that I've had to remember time and time again that no matter how unfortunate I am now, I mean, I'm fortunate now, but on where's unfortunate then, I could always change it. It's my change in my appearance and these guys could, I know that sounds like it's not that important, but it is. It is. There's nothing they can do. But now, you know, we're at a time in the late 1990s in which you have, who supports the Negro Leads right now? It's not the blacks. It's unfortunately, it's most of the guys that they receive support from are white. And there are some that are in the Negro Leads. They're wearing it like Joe Black that hate that. I've been asked and embarrassed a number of times by certain very vocal blacks about that. Need these guys along. We blacks will take care of them. I mean, here we are in 1998 and I still hear that. That's very strange. It's not like, it's not the picture that most people want to hear or see, you know? And I understand, you know, why it's indeed shocking to be told, I know what I've been told this. I know what you've done for them. I know it's caused you an arm and a leg and I know you've lost a lot of friends and had a lot of disturbed sleep over this and that's all true. But don't do it anymore. It's not necessary. If you're going to do it, find some black person to do it. Well, this is like to me like the end of the line. You can't, you know, hide after a while, you know, but that's not how the black players feel. That's how people who are quote some of the pillars in the black community feel that way. They're almost like, in certain sense, like Yasser Arafat is, you know, that's the end point. Maybe a little, it's the most people that may be a little bit cruel and hard, but it is a strange thing in which we have attempted many times to get the black community involved in and part of it is that just don't have the money. I went to a group of...

segments.text: That's when I think most people forget, while you're donating your time to pay for these
things and to set up the different, whether it's going to be an auction, whether it's going
to be a show or something like that, it's an immense amount of money to pay other people
to do your work and that's never counted.
I mean, that's the most expensive part.
It comes into, I hate to get them because whenever I've discussed this with the general
family, most of the people within the family thought we were crazy, they thought we were
fools.
They would say, who's paying you to do this?
I mean, you know, when I work at something and I get paid $150-$200 an hour and I'm giving
not like 10 hours, I'm giving two months straight of using every chip, you know what
chit, it's me, you know, using every angle and every, everyone that ever owed me anything.
I would go to them and say, look, I did this for you, I did that for you and that and that.
I don't want anything for myself, but what about can you do this?
So you use up all those favors, which means that three years later, when you need them
again, you can't call them on it, nothing is free in politics and especially in the corporate
world, things that you do for other people are never forgotten, costs are not forgotten.
You know, I wish I would have known this when I was growing up.
I thought people did things out of it because they loved doing it with all the goodness
of their heart or that it was the right thing to do.
Seth, that's not how it was.
How it was is that, okay, Bob, I'll do that, I'll get that on there, but how about in return
I have this big house and I got lots of wall space for paintings.
Now I can't afford your work, but you know, then they'd let it drop.
You pick up the ball.
Okay, what do you want?
What do you want?
They won't spell it out.
You come over to my house and my studio and I'll show you around with the full intention
of knowing that when they get to a certain part of your studio, they're going to look at
your paintings, but it's never spoken.
Oh, I like that.
You like that a lot?
Oh, I like that very much.
Oh, okay.
Well, I'll tell you what, why don't you take it with you?
Okay, I'll take this with me.
Okay, you can be sure that, oh yes, of course we'll do that on the phone.
We'll make the phone calls, we'll do that.
You know, unfortunately, most people who did not go into these rat infested, roach infested,
no food, cold, squalor of places, and this wasn't Leon's place, these were other places
that I had gone into.
How in a world can you not want to help these people?
You know, we humans are like that.
We all feel that when you're in a situation like that, it's up to you to do something about
it, whether it's sweeping their floor or setting their chairs up straight or pouring them
a cup of coffee, you feel like you got to do something.
And unfortunately, that's dying in this country and, you know, because everyone's thinking
that when you get back, because we're going to run out of time if we don't do it, when
we get back to this alleged pension, of course the people who really talk about this are
the vice president and the president of NLBPA, they're the only really official speakers
on that.
And that is Wilmer Fields and Stanley Glenn.
But what we had thought was going to happen was this.
We had fought since, because these guys have been hammering at baseball for a long time,
you know, the organization baseball.
And we had thought that they would eventually cave in and say, okay, enough's enough.
You've proven that, you know, you had guys that hit 80 home runs a year, you had guys
that stole, could steal our base runners blind and strike out whole sides of our teams,
you know, and all that kind of thing.
So, all right.

It would never stop because all of a sudden some, some body who played on a black ball
team for a year or two, is elevated to the position of Leon Day.
Well, that would be the same thing as if you look at back in here as, oh, I don't know,

You match him up to a Hoyt Wilhelm or something.
I mean, you know, there's a lot of difference between greatness and good enough to make

And that's the same with the Negro League players.
They felt very strongly that the people that should receive these pensions, if you could

Well, when the Negro League, excuse me, black ball players got wind of money coming.
Well, it all helped loose because it drew the worst type of feelings out of individuals

That doesn't work that way.

Chances are it's probably closer to 150.

So you can imagine, and that's escalating.
So it's not going to be like six to ten years.
It's going to be like, and within ten years, it's all over.
They aren't going to be any of these players, whether they were major league, minor league,
mixed no difference.
So that difference in trying to get funds for those 170, we only succeeded in getting

even minor league.
They were called semi-pro.
How would you say that your involvement with the Negro league players has affected the
Baltimore community?
Oh, the Baltimore community.
I don't know how it's affected the Baltimore community.
I know how it's affected the national community.
See we've never had much help here in Baltimore.
It's really strange.
Most of the like some of the articles you'll see here written in the Dallas Times and
then they were mostly national because I was talking to well educated sports casters,
guys that had, believe it or not, there were not any broadcasters, but they had their
bachelor's and masters in the area of broadcasting services.
They were intelligent.
So when they got a book, they read it.
So we would send them a book on the history of the Negro league.
And I was so naive to think, oh, I want to send this $35 book to this person.
A week later, I get a call back from a guy in Dallas and he's like, damn, it's the greatest
gift I ever got.
I just finished it.
I'm talking about my sons and my dad and dad and that.
Well, you can imagine how it feels to finally attract those types.
Mel Allen was his name.
That's who it was.
You know, I heard him Mel Allen.
When I mentioned, he was from New York, Yankees.
He was one of the guys that used to think Negro leaders didn't do too much then after
he was educated.
We realized that they were just tops, you know, at least equal to.
Anyway, the Baltimore community, what we have done is we have literally been able to
bring a certain part of the Blacks history, which they are very proud of into the mainstream.
And so that we have certainly affected the Afro-American papers.
Baltimore son papers, the help that we've received there was not from the male
sports people.
It was the female in the son papers that were writing in the sports section.
They were the people that stood up for us.
And the reason why they stood up for was they said, this is a human situation.
They hit it right on the head.
How can you have a player go out on the field and play for a million dollars a year who's
a Black player and that's not very much these days, is it?
How can you have that guy go out there and do that, built on the backs of all these
other Black players that made it possible for him to do so?
To me, you've got to be pretty much of a slob.
You've got to be pretty much of a pig to do that to other humans.
But unfortunately, people like Reggie Jackson and others are like that.
You know, it was very unfortunate from that standpoint.
So as far as Baltimore goes, I think we educated the general community.
We certainly have not endeared herself to the sports people.
Are there any anecdotes that it's to go out in your mind from any of the players that
you can readily remember collecting?
Oh, yeah.
Most of the things that touched me deeply is when we took these guys to the White House,
and then we took them to the White House twice.
And it was all the way to the White House.
I mean, you have to understand these guys have never been recognized for anything.
And to go to the White House was the biggest dream they've ever had.
I mean, none of them ever fought.
None of them ever fought, but this would ever happen.
And on the way there, they would say, imagine this.
I'm going to go on to the White House.
I'm going to tell my wife all about it.
I'm like, my grandchildren, all of his grandchildren, they're going to have a big party when they
come back home so they can share what the White House was like.
Grandpa's going to the White House.
Well, then it's then that he started to reminisce.
And they'd say, a whole discussion's of, do you remember that game?
Or when we were on the way to someplace in the South, and the bus turned over?
And a lot of people were hurt, but no one was really savagely hurt.
And we put a phone call in and saying, hey, we're in real trouble here.
We're hurt.
The bus turned in the first thing, the owner of the team says to them was, hey, you've
got a half hour to get to the game.
Get to the game.
That's extraordinary.
Of course, there were major league players that played with broken thumbs.
And these guys, praying with broken ribs all the time, they couldn't, they had that kind
of pressure.
Or when they were hungry, this is the stuff that really got me because Seth, I come from
a very poor background.
Food has always been important to me.
I ate well when I was younger, but when I left home on my own, about when I was about
And I went through a period of time in which I had won me a little day for years.
I mean, that's the way it was.
So they would tell stories about the places they would go to eat, which I think was really
an incredible experience.
They would go to, of course, they could never go into front door.
They go around the back door, but they still had to go through the situation in which the
people behind the counter, even though they were feeding them, they would give them the
worst of what they had, stuff that was already bad.
But one story they told, which I just loved, went kind of like this.
And they went to this restaurant, restaurant, diner.
Can you call who these people are telling you?
Oh, yes, Monty Urban, Monty Urban, Buck Leonard, threw in a couple of stories there, but especially
Monty Urban, Jean Benson, Maillam Duckett, these individuals were all involved in this
particular one, Wilmer Fields.
So they sent one of the guys in who had, they always sent the guy in that had the lightest
complexion.
He would get the less hassle.
He would take the order and carry it out.
So they ordered something like, I don't know, 20 hand-borders or something like that,
20 sandwiches.
And so when he was at the counter and the white man looked at him and he said, okay, well,
here's these sandwiches, but you can't eat them here.
You have to take these out and go eat them elsewhere.
And I can't recall the exact words, but what they did was this.
He said, okay, you enjoy these 20 hand-borders, we're leaving.
And they left.
That was the funniest, the most fun they had in playing any, but usually it was just
the reverse.
It was usually like kind of like they would pull into a place to get a drink of water.
This was Monty Urban's story again.
And he would look out, he would look out back and there was a well, the water and the
person, of course, wouldn't give him anything to drink, wouldn't sell them sodas and everything.
And he said, well, can we have a drink from the well back there?
I mean, she said, okay, I guess so.
So they went back there, they used this little board of the ladle and they drank from the
ladle and they were kind of hurried by this lady.
They got in their cars and they looked out the window and as they were relieving, they
saw her in the back smashing the board.
Basically saying, I had to give these guys water, but I don't want to touch anything, these
guys.
Most of the traveling they did on the bus, they ate terribly.
They had these, many times they had these little box lunches, if you would recall that,
which each one had, it was pretty much the same thing.
They would go to a place they'd buy a loaf of bread and a poundable only and some onions
and few other things and maybe some drinks.
And they would store these under their seats.
Well, in 95 degree weather, they were eating food which was green.
They were haver, but they had to.
It's traveling from place to place without food was terrible, but staying in the places
that they had to stay in, none of us would ever stay in places like that.
Can you describe anything?
They actually describe some of the places that there were so many bed bugs and other vermin
within the bed that they had to leave the lights on.
There was a law that they had, a rule that they had.
Leave the lights on because the roaches in the bed bugs don't come out until you turn
the light off because when you turn the light off, and this is what I learned when I was
very poor and I used to have to sleep on the floor, if you leave the light on, the rats
and the other bugs.
I mean, it's not fun laying in bed and feeling roaches is crawling on you.
I've done it.
I know what it's like.
It is a miserable feeling, you know, and they had to do it all frequently because they
were sleeping in urine-infested beds in places that certainly would be condemned.
And the thing that I find most irritating is that as much as I like Buck O'Neill, who's
done a lot of good for the Negro League players, a lot, he has also hurt them in a sense that
he has come out and said many times that they stayed in the best hotels.
They ate the best food and dad and dad and dad and dad.
Well, not 99 percent of them.
Maybe the Kansas City monarchs did because they had things specially worked out.
You know, there are people Wilkinson, who was one of the owners of that time, was very,
very careful about his team.
So they would put them up at the best black hotels, eating the best black restaurants.
That's what Buck O'Neill neglected to say.
These were not top-notch anything.
They were, they may have been considered to be passable by whites, but for most blacks,
it was as good as they've was ever going to get.
So it was staying in hotels and, you know, the transportation thing, I think, has been
talked about often.
But how in a world can you stay in a bus for 16 hours straight?
Get out, get to the game one time and then go immediately into playing and beating the
team, then get into bus again traveling two hours to another place to go.
And then getting into the bus again for a third game.
They were playing three and four games a day.
Why?
Not because they loved it.
They had to make money.
And if they lost, they're not cut into their savings.
So they had to keep this incessant moving.
It reminds me of a lot of my friends in the rock and roll world who, when they go out
on these tours, it is miserable.
It's not any kind of life that anyone, anyone would choose because there's no time to do
anything serious.
There's no time to enjoy yourself.
You just work and work and work.
It was the same way with these individuals who had to, if they were strong enough, what
they were going to have to do is play another game if they could.
So it wasn't so much just the places where they stayed, but you can imagine hell.
And the guys, you can tell me about the buses.
The reason why I mentioned this thing about bus and the White House is because there
was the greatest bus these guys have ever been on.
WJZ gave us their VIP bus, which has lounges and places to lay down and places to eat.
And then go into the White House and things like that.
They would all say, can you imagine?
Because some of the teams didn't have, you're going to go swimming?
Some of the teams couldn't afford buses so they would use big cars.
They'd have to fit 15 people into two where they can set up to be Packards or older cars
to go from place to place.
And these were the cars that would turn over a lot.
Because they'd be riding down in the South, I understand, when they didn't have cement
asphalt type roads, they would have dirt roads.
And then these dirt roads, what happens is if you ride down the same ruts all the time,
you have a serious problem.
You have ruts along the side and this mound that builds up in the center.
So if you were riding down this road and your wheels hit the wrong angle on these little
hills that were in the middle, you would turn your car over.
And this happened not a lot.
But dozens of times, the most people would say that's a lot.
But I mean, since they were traveling infinitely all the time, the southern roads were really
the worst, as well as the way they were treated by the people in the towns.
With the exception of just a few people, like Satjel Page, he was the only one that could
get away with what he was, because he was like, whenever he came to town and pitched for
a team, and I know you already know this, the team was going to make money.
The whites were going to make money.
The people, the concession stands were going to make money because more people would come.
He was a major drawing card all the time.
And of course, he would pitch some games, he would pitch one inning, some games he would
pitch three innings.
At the end of his career, that's what he would do.
So the people would say, Satjel Page is pitching, we're going to the game.
Well, he would pitch one inning and that would bring out the other individuals who may
have even been better than Page, but couldn't draw.
Because if you couldn't draw on those days, we didn't have any big ads, you know, you
didn't have any TV.
It was the people on the stands that Page are free.
So you got to had to draw.
If you didn't draw, it was over.
I think one of the things I have learned from them, though, Seth, is we'll never see other
human beings like these players ever again.
Because of the way the media has played up all sports stuff.
These guys were heroes in the sense that they, not only were the better than everybody
else that they were playing against or at least equal to, but they knew how to take crap.
They knew how to be humiliated and not show it.
It's built up something within their character that we'll never see again.
People aren't like that.
Blacks, whites, reds, makes no difference.
Not that we really should be that way, but they had this patience which no longer exists.
And part of some people say, yeah, they had patience because if they did the wrong thing,
they'd get their head blown off.
Well, yeah, that's true.
But they also had very spiritual lives.
And when you go talk with Buck Leonard and Buck O'Neill and others, when they travel
in these buses, Wilmer Fields and Stanley Glen and Benson and Maelan and all these
other guys, they were all, they all had a spiritual, most of the ones that I had, and the affiliated
and worked with, had this deep spiritual thing about Christ.
No two ways about it, but they felt very close to Christ and they would sing all the time.
They'd sing gospels all the time.
Well, we were going to the White House.
Just to remember, they were flashing back thinking, God, the bus that we had then, we had
three good tires and one that we wrapped with rags, the whole together or something like
that.
And here we are going to the White House with a brand new VIP bus.
It was just like, I think it added years on to their life because they almost all have
the same health kind of problems, prostate cancer and certainly bad hearts because of
their diet.
You know, everything was deep fried.
They were told that was good for you except for a central page.
He said, you know, you never should eat this kind of stuff because it'll kill you.
Kill your gun.
I don't know how he learned that or who told him it.
He was right.
He was one of the few ball players that took care of himself physically in the kind of
food he ate.
What didn't mean that he was not trying to build this kind of a saint because he wasn't.
He had a mean streak like everybody else and just like our president was probably a
big womanizer as, you know, as a gentleman is and probably always will be.
So where we go from here?
I would ask you what impact has this had on your life with the new game players?
Well, it's kept me in touch with what it is like never to be accepted.
You see, I'm white.
Being white, I can go places that other humans can't.
I know that now.
I've forgotten what I was like.
I remembered back in the late 60s, if you ever saw pictures of me, I had a beard that
was down to here.
My hair was screaming all.
I was considered to be being an artist at that time since you were very poor, unacceptable
as a human.
I know what it's like to go into places where you're beat up or things are thrown at you.
You try, you know, my car was bashed up a number of times by Rednecks and whites who
would see your painted car or painted auto and they think, I want to destroy this bastard.
I don't care.
You know, and being a small person, you're an easy hit.
But I'm still white.
So from that standpoint, I could get my hair cut.
I could get a shave.
I could drive, use ordinary transportation and I could melt right back into society.
These guys never could.
And it's one of the things that I've had to remember time and time again that no matter
how unfortunate I am now, I mean, I'm fortunate now, but on where's unfortunate then, I could
always change it.
It's my change in my appearance and these guys could, I know that sounds like it's not
that important, but it is.
It is.
There's nothing they can do.
But now, you know, we're at a time in the late 1990s in which you have, who supports
the Negro Leads right now?
It's not the blacks.
It's unfortunately, it's most of the guys that they receive support from are white.
And there are some that are in the Negro Leads.
They're wearing it like Joe Black that hate that.
I've been asked and embarrassed a number of times by certain very vocal blacks about that.
Need these guys along.
We blacks will take care of them.
I mean, here we are in 1998 and I still hear that.
That's very strange.
It's not like, it's not the picture that most people want to hear or see, you know?
And I understand, you know, why it's indeed shocking to be told, I know what I've been
told this.
I know what you've done for them.
I know it's caused you an arm and a leg and I know you've lost a lot of friends and had
a lot of disturbed sleep over this and that's all true.
But don't do it anymore.
It's not necessary.
If you're going to do it, find some black person to do it.
Well, this is like to me like the end of the line.
You can't, you know, hide after a while, you know, but that's not how the black players feel.
That's how people who are quote some of the pillars in the black community feel that way.
They're almost like, in certain sense, like Yasser Arafat is, you know, that's the end point.
Maybe a little, it's the most people that may be a little bit cruel and hard, but it is
a strange thing in which we have attempted many times to get the black community involved
in and part of it is that just don't have the money.
I went to a group of...
